observations.summary: Some people respond to grief by discarding all remnants of what they loved, starting anew with nothing.

evidence.quote: Some people throw everything away when they lose the thing they love most—burn it all, scatter the ashes, start fresh with empty hands.

observations.summary: Others respond to grief by clinging tightly to every fragment of the lost, to the point of suffocation.

evidence.quote: Others keep every thread, every cracked vessel, every scrap of parchment with nothing written on it, and pull it tighter and tighter until there is no room left to breathe.

observations.notes: This claim contrasts with the previous, highlighting two opposing grief responses.

observations.summary: Aviel is characterized as someone who clings tightly to the remnants of what he has lost, embodying the second grief response.
observations.notes: This links Aviel's grief style to the motif of clinging tightly to loss.

observations.summary: Aviel's son is actively working to undo his father's tight grip on grief, symbolized by creeping through the dark to undo it.

evidence.quote: it is exactly what his son was creeping through the dark to undo.

observations.notes: This suggests a dynamic of healing or change between father and son.

accepted_observations.notes: This links Aviel's grief style to the motif of clinging tightly to loss.
